title: Khadijah Plummer -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Khadijah Plummer -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi, Khadijah.
Hi, good morning, Andi.
How are you?
Good.
How are you?
Doing well, thank you.
Where are you based?
I'm based in Cambridge, Ontario.
Oh, cool.
Awesome.
And yourself?
I'm in Rhode Island.
Oh, nice.
Both on the East Coast.
Yeah, amazing.
Cool.
Cool.
Well, thank you so much for scheduling some time.
Excited to chat and learn a little more about you.
Have you talked to Panzer already?
Yes, yeah, he was my last, my previous call.
Perfect.
So I'm guessing he gave you a little bit of a sense of the role.
Um, I can give you kind of my perspective on how I'm thinking about it.
Um, Yeah,
Hands are things a lot about like the content and the technical piece.
I am more focused on kind of the holistic, like how this role fits into like the whole of the org, thinking about like structure and kind of gaps for what we need.
But the, you know, we think of growthx as, you know, services agency, but we're built on the basis of a product, a platform that we're building and using internally to generate content.
Panzers in there, like, really focused on improving that platform piece.
So I'm sure you guys got a little bit of a chance to talk about that.
For sure.
But the, you know, this role is a mixture of a couple of things.
You know, obviously, strong editorial chops, the, you'll be working with a content manager, and a managing editor manages three to five accounts, and content managers,

And that's really running the workflows on our platform day to day, generating the content, but the managing editor is kind of really responsible for shaping and editing that content, making sure it's in a good place to go out, and then thinking about like just the general project management of the account.

So on a week-to-week basis, are we tracking to meet the engagement requirements and the scope of work, you know, communicating with the client on this is where deliverables are, and just like a little bit client-facing, but a lot of that project management piece.
And then on the other side, thinking about like helping improve the process and product itself, you know, part of editing is making sure that, you know, you can express and know what's good.
And so I think like being able to communicate that.

And help give feedback to the engineering team as we're improving these pipelines to make sure that there's like fewer and less and less work that we have to do on the back end after like the AI generates content.
So like not just editorial, but also being able to communicate kind of like what's good, what's working, things like that.
That makes sense.
Yeah, so I think the like the other piece that's important to note is you'll be working with like an account director and that person is like high level overseeing 10-ish accounts.
So you'll be, there will be a couple of managing editors in pod format formation under an account director working on like strategy relationship and like the long-term vision of the client.
And so, yeah, you're kind of like in the middle of everything.
But I think editorial is something that we're thinking about first and foremost, and like SEO strategy.
Yeah, so we'd love to hear like a little bit about you, get your elevator pitch, I'll ask you some questions, and then save time at the end for you to ask me some questions.
Okay, sounds good.
So in terms of my elevator pitch, I would say that I, after grad school, got into sales.
And then I was also working on the agency side.
These are both contract roles that was the sales and agency role that took me through the course of my first year, in the real world, you could say.
After that, I got into the tech space as an outbound sales rep.
And then within that same company, which was in the martech space, specifically, I moved into marketing, specifically content marketing, even though I sat in the product marketing pod.
And basically, from that point, I caught the marketing bug, and I pretty much haven't let go of it since.
So I
I moved through marketing generalist to content marketing specialist, content marketing manager, and then marketing lead, an agency specifically.

Not really being client-facing in any of my agency roles, except for my very first one, but more working on the brand and growth part of the agency's image itself, and working on things for share of voice, just coming up with a strategy on how to position themselves and grow in their respective markets that they were trying to move towards.
These days, I've been working in sales part-time at a Pilates studio.
I was working full-time at the start of the year, but after the election, they got rid of all their Canadian employees, so that wasn't really within my control and not within my planning.

But it's been good to kind of, I guess, lean on skills that I have built on previously in terms of sales, and still have the opportunity to work on content and marketing and then some operational things as well.

As I've been with the organization for a little while, but I know that I want to get back to what I enjoy and what I've, you know, I know I've been good at previously, which is what is leading me to kind of explore new roles and positions.
So that was a long elevator ride, but.
No, it's perfect.
I had somebody give me like a 15 minute one yesterday and I was like, we have 30.
Okay, fair.
That makes me feel better.
So thanks.
So yeah, don't worry about it.
Okay.
Yeah.
Well, I mean, talk to me about like the, the content that you've, that, or like how you approach content generation and maybe also how that's changed with like the advent of AI more and more.
Yeah, absolutely.
So I think before, not that I'm old or anything, but I definitely considered myself more of an old school marketer.
I mean, it's like three years, you know, like, and things have changed.
So you can still be pretty young and have lived through.
Yeah, that's fair.
That's fair.
So I think that moving towards AI has been an adjustment, but in a good way, as in it's been a new challenge.

But before I was, you know, the type to kind of make outlines based on topic research, using different tools like SEMrush, Ahrefs, you know, mapping out that content, kind of picking my focus keywords for whatever program, or whatever our themes are for the quarter, depending on how you want to put it.

And then basically making a workback schedule from there in terms of like, what needs to go out when, how many pieces that are more long form, maybe we have some video in there, things for social, how we're repurposing this content, how we're promoting it.
That's essentially, I guess, my flow of work that I used to do previously.

But with AI, it's been awesome to kind of use it to not only ideate, but also to kind of help on the management, the product management side, especially working with clients, like with freelance and stuff, because they were definitely moving more towards AI.
Thanks, guys.

So I kind had to force myself to get into it if I wanted to keep up, but it's proven really helpful, yeah, to just like stay organized, to kind of, yeah, dive deeper on versus like an idea versus like, not just knowing if the keywords or the topics or what other people are talking about, or even just like reading other competitors content, but just like, yeah, kind of gut checking in a way to see if it's a good idea or ideas worth pursuing in terms of like building out series and things like that.
But yeah, I would say my approach, I still feel like my comfort zone is more towards using all those extra steps I used to do in the past, but I've definitely found efficiency by using AI as well.
And I know that's how things are moving.
So I'm definitely trying to embrace it.
Okay, and like, how are you?
Are you using it for project management and planning?
Like you mentioned, like with AI?
Yeah, for sure.
So I wouldn't say like, I'm not like using like chat GPT specifically.
Specifically, that's one that everyone knows, but for project management, but like the AI tools within project management tools I've used in the past.
So I would say the one I've used the most, or I guess the two I've used the most, are probably Asana and Wrike.

And just using those, the capabilities within those to like, like map out the projects, also map out like the different touch points for stakeholders, you know, kind of even making project timelines specifically in Asana.

And then like assigning those points a lot faster than having to go through manually and seeing how much time we have for the project and just like assigning those tasks manually, just being able to use those to kind of keep things moving have been really helpful.
So awesome.
I think that's huge is like being able to leverage AI in the tools to like leverage.
seems like it's such an unlock.
I think so.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Can you talk to me about a time like as you've been generating content strategy, when you've had to like shift things?
Thinking and, you know, come up with, like, something's changed quickly, and you've had to change your approach to content strategy?
Yeah, for sure.

So one, like said, my recent example, I would say was creating a white paper that was focused on people in the healthcare space, not necessarily like healthcare workers, even though that's who we were gathering the data from for this white paper, but more so folks that are in, like, the HR space and, like, employer branding space to basically make, like, healthcare recruitment and culture better for those who are working in it and, like, attract more talent to that space.
So we did a survey that we ran ourselves, and we partnered with Gartner on that at the organization I was with.
And then we then had, like, our first iteration of the white paper after we disseminated all the data, decided which questions we wanted to focus on.
And then kind of wrote that first narrative.
But then after we went through it with other...
professionals, like we kind of like, I guess, gut checked with folks that were already in that space in terms of like CHROs in healthcare.
And we realized that the data that they were concerned about was not necessarily reflected in the white paper.
So we obviously weren't going to rerun the survey, because that would be costly.
But it was just like, how could we emphasize certain points that would actually be important and relevant in the best way possible for folks in that space.
So it was like a month long process.
But I feel like that was a big pivot point.
And we still didn't want to shift our deadline.

So it was just like, you know, getting all hands on deck, figuring out how we wanted to then promote it, because we had promotion plans in place, we had the paid advertising kind of copy and everything just like ready to go.

So just like, getting all the different pieces together, all the different stakeholders that were involved, to move quickly on shifting towards the narrative that we all agreed upon after talking with our stakeholders.
Of course, learning from that is to get folks like that involved much earlier in the process.
Um, but we thought that what we were seeing was very clear and like what we wanted to read versus like what would actually make sense for our audience at that time.
So that was a good lesson.
And we were able to still make our deadline.
So that was a good feeling, too.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Um, so we still use like SEMrush, Ahrefs, things like that.
So talk to me about like your process and generating strategy and how you think about like setting strategy for clients long term.
Ooh, that's a good question.
And just so you know, like, yeah, I think this, I don't know why this doesn't come across clearly.
But like, we don't think about like, podcasts or social or like, we're really like blog content, like core.
I mean, newsletters are like even.
A little bit outside of what we do.
So just, like, think about it in that, in that.
Okay.
Okay, awesome.
So how I think about strategy, right?
That was the question?
Sorry.
Sorry.
So, like, really about how you approach, like, content strategy, like, when setting a strategy for, you know, a new client or engagement or, like, thinking about setting strategy.
And so, yeah.
Like, and we do, like, so much Ahrefs, but, like, what's your normal process?
Okay, for sure.
No, that's really helpful.
Okay, so basically, in the way that I've always been taught and then adopted when it comes to doing this content strategy is to not, like, think too far ahead.
Not in the sense, like, I don't mean, like, oh, you can't plan for, like, the year or anything like that, but it's just, like, thinking about it in those smaller, manageable pieces.
And then usually, like, I...
Like to do it on a quarterly basis, but it all rolls into like a larger theme, or idea, or program, or whatever word you want to use.
So that's definitely an approach that I take, kind of thinking about the whole year, but then how, like, what is it, what is the message that we're trying to build on from Q1 through to Q4?
And then from that point, planning out how many pieces of content, the cadence of the content.
I know we're not talking about, like, shorter form things, but like, how are we also, like, promoting that on social media is something that's often fallen into my purview.
But just thinking about it as like a quarterly theme, or program.
And then from that point, looking at what competitors in your similar space are saying about that specific program idea, topic.
And then using that to then kind of inspire the topic research from there, and then just focusing on, like, which, let's say, like, five keywords we're going to use.
And.
Focus on and make sure we lace through everything, of course, without getting too repetitive.
So that might change on a quarterly basis, or maybe we swap out two of the five for something else that is more relevant or will strengthen the points from the previous quarter.
But it's like thinking about the whole holistic view for the marketing year or the fiscal year, whatever, however you're following that calendar, and then breaking it up into its quarterly program.
So that's often my approach.
Yeah, so you're thinking about like, like five keywords per quarter, or yeah, over the course.
Okay, I mean, per quarter, like three to five.
How are you like generating content in that instance?
Like, what's your generation process once you get to the strategy piece?
Like, like how to actually create the content?
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Okay, yeah.
So from that point, once we have like, our topics slash keywords locked in, then we're going to come up with like titles for the.
They could just be working titles, of course.
And then assigning what type of piece it would be.
So for example, we might do like five blog posts on, I don't know, building a community, for example.
So we might be like, you know, how to create a community online, how to set up a community in X tool, and so on.
have like our list of things.
And then we, between like the, I guess, more top of funnel or like educational content, we're like, okay, what do we need to then strengthen those things?
So then it's like, okay, we have, we're working in the community space.
Let's just go with that example.
It's like, okay, like, let's have like one to two case studies that fall within that.
And then we're like, okay, who do we want to reach out to in terms of our customers that we want to actually focus on and write these stories?
When do we want to release them?
And then put that within the calendar.
And then it's like, we're also thinking about the shorter pieces of content.
like taking key nuggets of data, and then, you know, releasing them on social.
And that falls into the map as well, the content map of release.
So that's how, once you have, like, your list of, like, working pieces, then we go in to actually create the content from there.
Yeah.
So that's, like, kind of the plan, I guess, or the planning process.
I don't know if answered your question, but I can go further.
Okay.
Sorry.
No, that's okay.
Just taking notes.
Yeah.
So are you using, like, how would you use AI to generate this content, too?
Because I think, like, one of the things that, and this is totally fine, like, we're, you know, at sort of an inflection point here, but we're generating five pieces of content per week for clients.
So, like, keywords and things like that are, like, at a much higher scale as we're generating strategy.
Of course.
So, you know, how would you think about building leverage for yourself, thinking that you're going to have to be, like, focusing on...
On, like, content strategy for three months that's gonna have, like, 60 to 100 pieces of content in it for a client.
Yeah.
That's a great question, but one I don't really know how to answer because I've never faced that challenge.
But I guess my first thought, just, like, knowing what I know, would be to just leverage the tools that we have in-house.

Um, but I don't know how to think about it in terms of, like, yeah, that many keywords because if you're doing that many pieces per week, like, that's obviously gonna get way too repetitive if have too small of keyword list.
Um, I have generated, like, CSVs as well using SEMrush, which give you, like, way more keywords, but, like, the strength of those keywords, um, might not be the best.
And, like, there's a lot of manual filtering that has to go, um, that has to be done.
So, I don't know if that's even the best thing to do.
Um, but.
but.
but.
Okay.

But yeah, I'm really honestly not sure how to answer that other than using the tools around me and the people around me that can help me to generate more keywords to then be able to scale that content process.
But yeah, that's not really a great answer, but I'm not really sure how to go for it.
Yeah, mean, it's a learning curve, I guess.
Yeah, we have 10 minutes left.
I think like, here's where I am.
I like your background.
I think like you'll be good editorially.
Like, I think, yeah, I'm thinking is good.
But we're really like leverage is going to be super, super important as we expand.
Like we're not a traditional agency where we were growing.
of course.
So, like, I'd like to move you forward.
But the thing that we're going to be looking at when we're in this next exercise is like...
How you're generating content using AI and whether you can, like, make quality content using AI.
Yeah, that's fair.
I mostly have used it for, like, out or, like, outlines or topics, but, like, not in terms of, like, doing a large volume of content, but simply because I haven't had to yet.
I I would love the challenge, but I understand as well if it's not, like, fitting.
Yeah, I mean, so, like, you did the exercise, right?
I did, yeah.
What was your process of doing the exercise?
I didn't use any AI.
Maybe I should have.
Okay, yeah, I mean, to us, that's a red flag.
Like, we need, it's a 90-minute exercise because we really want somebody that's going to, like, we're finding that people that are, like, not using AI at all are struggling.
Um, terms of, like, success in the role.
Um, because there's so.
Much work.
Right.
Yeah, of course.
So, yeah, I think I still only took like about an hour and 20 minutes to do it.
But yeah, I didn't know I was close to because I wasn't using like your tool.
So I didn't really want to do that.
But maybe I should have done that, I guess would have been a lot.
Yeah, I mean, I think like, I have to take another look at the instructions and maybe they're not super clear.
Um, yeah, I'm wondering, like, I, like I said, I like your background.
I'm thinking that the next step might be if we like, are you open to freelance work?
I am.
Yes.
Okay.
I do have my own like sole proprietorship and such.
So yeah.
Yeah.
So maybe we like we have done this in the past where we've like engaged.
We had another person that that did like, was like, I didn't use any AI.
And we're like, well, we need to like, see that in order to.
Yeah, that wasn't even.
I totally would have done that.
feel like it's so dumb now, but...
No, no, that's okay.
Yeah, so I think, like, I'm gonna have Marilia reach out to you about possibly doing some freelance editing.
Let her know your rates, and we'll figure that out.
And that means that you'll have a chance to, like, do the work on a week-to-week basis, and, like, our managing editors can evaluate that kind of output.
Okay.
Um, and, like, like, there's, it's a really interesting challenge that we're in.
like, giving you a lot of behind-the-scenes here.
No, that's okay.
I appreciate it.
Um, because, um, like, there is a lot of, like, manual editing.
Like, you're not necessarily using AI to edit, but there is a piece also, like, our platform is, there's still, like, editorial work or, like, some, some work that happens.
Like, in Claude or in, like, perplexity.
Mm
That helps supplement kind of the content generation piece.
And so there will be opportunities for that.
And like, you know, I asked a lot of questions about like project management and content strategy, because those are also aspects of the role.
But it's leaning into like AI on the content side is, it's just an unlock, like, because otherwise you're going to be like, literally manually moving, like, like rewriting content.
And that just takes so, so long, because it's really hard.
And, you know, like I said, we're producing content at scale.
So I think I'm going to have Marilia send you, like, reach out about freelance.
And, you know, like, the last time it was like a two month evaluation process, and then we kind of made a decision.
And, you know, we did extend an offer.
So it's not like.
Oh, okay.
It's just like, just do this freelance work.

Like, I think if we do good work, there is an opportunity, but I just don't want to go through the panel, because the questions that we'll be asking are about, like, your engagement with AI in the process.
That's fair.
Honestly, I really appreciate that.
Yeah, I'll make sure that those instructions are a little bit clearer.
Yeah.
I, um, yeah.
Yeah.
I'm going to update the, yeah.
Sounds good.
Uh, yeah.
And I mean, like, if we moved you on, it's because, like, your content was good.
Um, yeah.
So, you know, like, as I said, it's only been three years.
Like, a lot of people have not moved to, like, being fully AI native in their content generation.
So, you need both.
I hear that.
I feel that.
Okay.
That makes sense.
Yeah.
So,
I think there's an opportunity here, it just might be a little bit longer, and hopefully there's some work in it for you in the short term, too.
Yeah, well, I appreciate that, rather than just being like, okay, here's the door.
No, I mean, like, that's really, yeah, I think it's a lot.
Yeah, yeah, okay, cool.
Well, thanks for your time, Khadijah.
Oh, we do have four minutes left, so if you want to ask me any questions, like, what can I answer for you?
Okay, sure.
So, I see that you're, like, on the customer ops side, so how, like, how does that connect to this role, exactly?
Like, I can kind of see the vision, but, like, internally, what does that look like?
Wait, sorry, say that again.
Like, so, the managing editor position, and then your role in customer ops, like, how do those interface, I guess, like, internally?
Um, so, like, we, um...
Um...
We set our org up in, we have a build team, like engineering team that does, like, all of the works on the product itself.
Then we have a deliver team.
And I guess, like, we think of, my background's in, like, customer success and account management.
So I'm, but I'm thinking about, like, the sort of org structure and how we scale our teams.
And, like, so I'm, I guess I'm, like, I manage all of the account directors who manage the managing editors.
So, so, like, I kind of, like, lead the team and I don't have a marketing background.
But because of, because this is kind of a mix of, like, CS product and, and, like, the marketing piece, and, like, leveraging AI and systems thinking, that's, yeah, that's how I fit in.
I know it's, like, a weird title for.
What, like, what we do, but that's the confusion of being in, like, sort of, like, a product-led marketing agency.
Yeah, okay, but that makes sense now.
Thanks for explaining that.
And then another question I had, I'm trying to think now, back to the JD, there was something, what was it?

Oh, so, like, so the workflow piece, like, are we also, like, maybe it's not to this role, but do we, like, do the workflows, like, you said the content managers use the workflows to generate the content, but is it also something that, like, customers or clients are ever using, like, in conjunction with us, or, like, seeing, like, what our process is in relation to what we're, like, what our output is for them?
I don't know if that makes sense.
Yeah, so we are giving them, so, like, the brief that you...
And like brand voice and stuff, all of that gets put into the AI, we call it artifacts, but it's sort of like informational documents.
And then AI is like, it's, we're like, leveraging different LLMs at different stages to do like deep research, and then to like generate an article, and then to do fact checking.
And then it's like, okay, does this align with the brand voice?
Are there specific writing guidelines that it needs to check against?
And so we do show the client like the, like the steps in the workflow, and some of the inputs like that they can control.
So we can say things like, only check, like, if you're like a tax website, like only check like.gov for sources and references or something like that.
Okay.
So we show them like the high level, like what the inputs are, but they're not in the product itself today.
Okay.
Okay, that's great to know.
Yeah, I will.
I figured that it was mostly us that would be running it, but I was still, I was wanting to clarify that.
So I appreciate that.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
So, yeah, I think, like, the content managers, it depends.
Some people run the workflows themselves, like, we're sort of in an in-between phase.
But, yeah, so you're not, like, writing, like, the AI prompts every day, but there is, like, some AI prompting and stuff that happens.
Like, the artifact generation, like, AI can do all that, like, competitor research and all that stuff.
So, like, that's what we have to do.
And so, like, that's just, like, less time.
Yeah, makes it more efficient, for sure.
Okay.
Well, that's great to know.
Okay, those were my main questions.
I feel like between the last two calls, I've gotten a lot of info, so I appreciate it.
yeah.
Yeah, thank you.
Cool.
I mean, I really like talking to you, so, yeah.
Yeah, likewise.
We'll chat again.
Once we get.
You set up in the freelance space.
Thanks so much, Andi.
I appreciate you.
Thanks, Khadijah.
Bye.
All right.
Bye.

## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview with Khadijah Plummer for Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=35.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role involves editorial work, project management, and improving AI content generation processes](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=101.0)
  - [Khadijah has strong editorial background but limited experience with AI content generation at scale](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=1164.0)
  - [Next steps: Explore freelance opportunity to evaluate Khadijah's AI-assisted content skills](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=1287.0)

## Topics

### Role Overview

  - [Mix of editorial, project management, and process improvement](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=101.0)
  - [Managing editor oversees 3-5 content managers and accounts](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=101.0)
  - [Responsible for shaping/editing content, project management, and client communication](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=101.0)
  - [Works with account director overseeing \~10 accounts](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=202.0)
  - [Focus on blog content, not podcasts/social media](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=745.0)

### Khadijah's Background

  - [Experience in sales, agency work, and content marketing](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=259.0)
  - [Progressed from marketing generalist to content marketing specialist/manager](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=292.0)
  - [Strong editorial skills but limited experience with AI-assisted content generation at scale](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=1164.0)
  - [Comfortable with traditional content strategy and SEO tools (SEMrush, Ahrefs)](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=429.0)

### Content Strategy Approach

  - [Khadijah plans quarterly themes tied to yearly goals](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=814.0)
  - [Focuses on 3-5 keywords per quarter](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=877.0)
  - [Creates content calendar with mix of blog posts, case studies, etc.](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=936.0)
  - [Limited experience generating high volumes (60-100 pieces/quarter) with AI assistance](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=1050.0)

### AI Integration Concerns

  - [Khadijah didn't use AI for take-home exercise, raising concerns](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=1193.0)
  - [GrowthX heavily relies on AI for content generation (5 pieces/week/client)](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=1050.0)
  - [Success in role requires embracing AI tools for efficiency](https://fathom.video/share/FBzZ6Sa7i_xZKJkf22FHpkLAj7L8esNz?tab=summary&timestamp=1389.0)
